Adversarially review a generated HLD session. Be a skeptic — find REAL defects, review-only, DO NOT modify files.

Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/143dbb94-126b-429e-a1d7-c615d7708804_hld/_response.md (+ maybe capacity.py). Problem: Design a Real-Time Driver Heatmap System. Emphasis: geo-bucketing (S2 cells) + windowed aggregation of the location firehose, the read path (precomputed tiles), update frequency vs cost, hot dense cells.

Report PASS/FAIL with evidence on each:
1. ALL 11 sections present and fully expanded; md > 20,000 chars. §1 must OPEN with the 🔒 scope-lock (forking assumptions FIRST), then derived functional requirements, then provisional NFRs. §2 must be the residual forks and must NOT re-ask anything §1 already locked (flag any duplication).
2. CAPACITY (§3): numbers actually derived + self-consistent (re-run capacity.py with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python if present); a named decision-forcing number + flip threshold; a peak-from-average burst factor; no false precision.
3. TECHNICAL CORRECTNESS for THIS problem + the emphasis: are the deep dives the genuinely hardest parts (binding bottleneck, hot-key, global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, back-pressure)? Find any wrong or hand-wavy claim.
4. MERMAID SAFETY (CRITICAL — many diagrams): scan EVERY ```mermaid block; FAIL any label/edge-text containing breaking chars — parentheses, a colon, a semicolon, slashes, quotes, angle brackets, curly braces. Report the exact offending line. Confirm flowchart TD for multi-path.
5. §5 has real REST endpoints + JSON + idempotency + object-level authz + versioning/pagination noted. §7 has 5-part storage cards + PART D data lifecycle. §9 covers back-pressure, observability, rate-limiting. §10 has CAP/PACELC line.
6. Markdown hygiene: no LaTeX, no stray tags, tables well-formed, emoji-script structure present.

Return verdict SHIP or FIX-FIRST + findings (severity/where/problem/fix). Mermaid breaking-char issues are BLOCKER. Only BLOCKER/MAJOR block.

content.content:
1	# Design a Real-Time Driver Heatmap System
2	
3	We're building the thing that paints the live "demand bubbles" on a ride-hail map — the colored blobs a driver sees that say *go here, it's hot*. The job: take a firehose of live driver and rider locations, bucket them into geographic cells, count them in a short rolling window, and push a fresh per-city heatmap tile to millions of map screens every few seconds. The whole tension is **a write-heavy firehose meeting a read-heavy map** — and making that cheap.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions
8	
9	> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one flow at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the location firehose, the precomputed read path, and hot dense cells — checking in with you as I go. I'll keep cost and operations first-class since that's where a heatmap actually lives or dies. Sound good?"
10	
11	The core tension in one line: **ingesting millions of location pings per second is a throughput problem, but the heatmap a user sees is a tiny, shared, cacheable artifact — so the art is decoupling the expensive write side from the cheap read side and never letting either starve the other.**
12	
13	### 🔒 Scope lock (assumptions stated as vetoable, before requirements)
14	
15	I'll commit to these four forking assumptions and let you veto any of them:
16	
17	1. **This is a *display* heatmap, not the matching/dispatch engine.** I'll assume the heatmap exists to *visualize* density for drivers and ops dashboards — it does NOT decide who gets matched to which rider. Stop me if you want the dispatch/ETA path too; that's a different system.
18	2. **It's approximate and eventually-consistent, refreshed every ~5 seconds.** I'll assume nobody needs an exact, transactionally-correct count — "about 40 drivers in this neighborhood, a few seconds old" is the product. If you actually need exact, audited counts, the design changes a lot.
19	3. **It's per-city / per-viewport, sharded geographically — not one global map.** I'll assume a user looks at one city's worth of map at a time, so I can shard the whole world by geography and never need a global join. Veto if you want a single worldwide zoom-out view as a hard requirement.
20	4. **Reads vastly outnumber the cost-per-read because tiles are shared.** I'll assume every viewer in a city sees the *same* tile, so the read side collapses to "build one tile per city, fan it out via CDN." Veto if heatmaps must be personalized per user (e.g. filtered by vehicle type) — that explodes the tile count.
21	
22	> 🤝 "Here's what I'm treating as in scope: ingest the location firehose, geo-bucket it, aggregate density in a rolling window, and serve precomputed per-city tiles to the map. Out of scope: actual matching, ETAs, surge *pricing* (we feed it, we don't compute it), and the rider-facing trip flow. Add or cut before I commit?"
23	
24	Because we assumed (2) approximate + (3) per-city + (4) shared tiles, the core flows fall out cleanly:
25	
26	### Functional requirements (above the line)
27	
28	**FR1 — Ingest the location firehose.** Accept a high-rate stream of driver (and optionally rider-demand) location pings.
29	- 🗣️ Plain words: every driver's phone says "I'm here" every few seconds, and we have to swallow all of those at once without dropping the floor out.
30	- ⚡ Why it's hard: it's millions of tiny writes per second — far more than any single database or single machine can take, so it has to be a buffered, sharded stream, not direct DB writes.
31	
32	**FR2 — Geo-bucket and aggregate into cell density.** Map each ping to a geographic cell and maintain a rolling count of drivers (and demand) per cell.
33	- 🗣️ Plain words: we chop the world into little tiles, and for each tile we keep a running tally of how many cars are sitting in it right now.
34	- ⚡ Why it's hard: doing a *windowed* count (last N seconds, not all-time) at firehose rate means in-memory aggregation with time-bucketing, and some cells (downtown at rush hour) are wildly hotter than others.
35	
36	**FR3 — Build & serve precomputed heatmap tiles.** On a fixed cadence, snapshot each city's cell densities into a compact tile and serve it to the map UI.
37	- 🗣️ Plain words: every few seconds we take a photo of the current density and hand the same photo to everyone looking at that city.
38	- ⚡ Why it's hard: millions of map screens want it, but if we precompute once per city and cache aggressively, the read cost is almost free — the trick is making the tile shared and cacheable.
39	
40	**FR4 — Let the client read the heatmap for a viewport / city / zoom.** Given where the map is centered and how zoomed in it is, return the right tile(s).
41	- 🗣️ Plain words: the app asks "what's the density around *here*, at *this* zoom?" and we hand back the matching colored cells.
42	- ⚡ Why it's hard: zoom level changes cell resolution, so we either store tiles at multiple resolutions or aggregate on the fly — and we must keep the read path latency tiny.
43	
44	**Below the line (out of scope):** driver-rider matching, ETA computation, surge *price* calculation, trip lifecycle/state machine, payments, the rider-side request flow, historical analytics/replay (we'd feed a separate warehouse), and personalized/filtered heatmaps.
45	
46	### Non-Functional requirements (provisional targets, tagged to assumptions)
47	
48	**NFR1 — Read latency p99 ~150 ms (ASSUMING shared per-city tiles, assumption 4).**
49	- 🗣️ Plain words: when the app asks for the heatmap, 99 times out of 100 it comes back in well under a fifth of a second.
50	- 💥 What breaks without it: the map "lags" behind the driver's real position; the demand blobs feel stale and drivers stop trusting them.
51	
52	**NFR2 — Freshness: tile reflects reality within ~5–10 seconds (ASSUMING approximate, assumption 2).**
53	- 🗣️ Plain words: the heat you see is at most about ten seconds behind where the cars actually are.
54	- 💥 What breaks without it: a driver chases a hot blob that emptied 30 seconds ago — they arrive to nothing and we've actively misled them.
55	
56	**NFR3 — Ingest durability is "lossy-OK," availability is not (ASSUMING approximate, assumption 2).**
57	- 🗣️ Plain words: dropping a *few* location pings is fine because the next one arrives in 4 seconds; but the *pipe* going down is not fine.
58	- 💥 What breaks without it: if we demand exactly-once durability on the firehose we'd pay 10x for a guarantee the product doesn't need — over-engineering.
59	
60	**NFR4 — Availability: read path 99.95% (~4.4 hrs/yr down), ingest 99.9%.**
61	- 🗣️ Plain words: the map almost never goes dark; the ingest can hiccup slightly more because it self-heals on the next ping.
62	- 💥 What breaks without it: drivers stare at a frozen or empty map during peak — exactly when the heatmap matters most.
63	
64	**NFR5 — Cost: read path must be near-free per viewer; the budget goes to ingest + egress.**
65	- 🗣️ Plain words: we can't pay real money every time someone glances at the map, so the same tile has to serve everyone.
66	- 💥 What breaks without it: at hundreds of thousands of reads a second, a per-read DB hit would cost a fortune and melt the database.
67	
68	> 💬 "The whole game here is that the writes are huge and the reads are huge, but the *answer* is tiny and shared — so I'll spend my engineering on swallowing the firehose and on caching one tile per city, and almost nothing on per-user read logic."
69	
70	---
71	
72	## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)
73	
74	These are the residual forks from the scope lock — the assumptions most likely to flip the architecture. I've already picked a branch for each; I'm surfacing them so you can redirect.
75	
76	**Fork A — I assumed approximate, eventually-consistent counts (every ~5s).**
77	- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
78	  - If you need **exact, audited density** (e.g. for regulatory driver-count reporting) → I can't aggregate-and-drop; I'd need a durable, replayable event log and exactly-once counting, roughly 5–10x the storage and a much heavier consumer.
79	  - If **approximate is fine** (my pick) → I aggregate in memory with windowed counters, never persist raw pings, and the firehose becomes cheap and lossy-tolerant.
80	- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "If you ever need these numbers to be *exact and auditable*, tell me — that's a different, much more expensive system; for a display heatmap I'm treating it as approximate on purpose."
81	
82	**Fork B — I assumed per-city geographic sharding, never a global join.**
83	- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
84	  - If a **single worldwide zoom-out** is a hard requirement → I'd need a hierarchical roll-up (city → region → continent tiles) and a cross-shard aggregation tier; doable but it adds a whole pre-aggregation layer.
85	  - If **per-city / per-viewport** is enough (my pick) → every shard is independent, there's no coordinator, and I can scale shards linearly by adding cities.
86	- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "I'm sharding the planet by geography so each city is independent — if you really want a live global zoom-out, I'll add a roll-up tier, but that's the one thing that reintroduces a coordinator."
87	
88	**Fork C — I assumed one shared tile per city, not personalized heatmaps.**
89	- ↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:
90	  - If heatmaps must be **filtered per driver** (only my vehicle class, only my service line) → the tile is no longer shared, CDN caching collapses, and read cost goes from ~free to per-user; I'd push filtering to the client or precompute a small fixed set of tile *variants* per city.
91	  - If **one tile per city** (my pick) → 99%+ CDN hit ratio and the read path is essentially a static-file CDN.
92	- 🗣️ How I'd say it: "The thing that makes reads almost free is that everyone in a city sees the same tile — if you need per-driver filtering, warn me, because that's what breaks the caching story."
93	
94	> 🤝 Checkpoint: "Those are the three places my design is most sensitive — approximate vs exact, per-city vs global, shared vs personalized. Happy with my picks, or do you want to flip one before I size it?"
95	
96	---
97	
98	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
99	
100	I computed these with a small `capacity.py`; I'll surface only the few that drive a decision. Gloss as I go: **QPS** = queries (or events) per second; **TTL** = time-to-live, how long a cached value stays valid; **S2 cell** = Google's scheme for chopping the globe into addressable square-ish tiles at many zoom levels.
101	
102	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
103	|---|---|---|---|---|
104	| Avg ingest (firehose) | 5M online drivers ÷ 4s ping interval = 1.25M/s | ~1.2M/s | "about a million pings a second" | Can't write to a DB — needs a stream + in-memory aggregation |
105	| **Peak ingest** | 1.25M/s × 2.0 burst (rain/surge) = 2.5M/s | **~2.5M/s** | "two and a half million a second at peak" | **THE binding bottleneck — forces sharded ingest** |
106	| Hottest single city | 50K drivers ÷ 4s × 2.0 burst = 25K/s | ~25K/s | "about 25 thousand a second downtown" | Hot-shard relief: sub-shard a hot city's cells |
107	| Peak read demand | 2M viewers ÷ 5s poll × 2.0 burst = 800K/s | ~800K/s | "around 800 thousand reads a second" | Must serve from CDN/edge, not origin |
108	| **Origin MISS QPS** | 800K/s × (1 − 0.99 hit) = 8K/s | **~8K/s** | "only about 8 thousand reach origin" | Origin sized from misses — a few nodes, not hundreds |
109	| Fresh tile builds | 1,000 cities ÷ 5s refresh = 200/s | ~200 tiles/s | "we build 200 tiles a second" | Tile build is cheap + fully decoupled from reads |
110	| Hot aggregate state | 20M occupied cells × 24 B = 0.48 GB | under 1 GB | "the live counts fit in under a gig" | Aggregate state lives in RAM (Redis), no disk |
111	| Raw-ping footprint IF persisted | 2.5M/s × 64 B × 86,400 × 30 × 3 repl × 1.5 WAL | ~1,900 TB/mo | "nearly two petabytes a month if we kept it" | We DON'T persist raw — aggregate-and-drop |
112	| **CDN egress cost** | 800K/s × 30 KB × time × $0.02/GB | **~$1.2M/mo** | "egress is the seven-figure line item" | **Top cost driver; tile size is the lever** |
113	| Unit economics | $1.2M/mo ÷ ~2T reads/mo | ~$0.60/1M reads | "sixty cents per million map views" | Cost is dominated by egress, not compute |
114	
115	**The ONE decision-forcing number:** the **~2.5M pings/s firehose**. A single Kafka-style partition tops out ~50K msg/s and one aggregator process handles maybe ~150K updates/s — so 2.5M/s **forces sharding the ingest by S2 cell prefix**. *Flip threshold:* below ~150K pings/s a single aggregator/Redis instance handles the whole thing and most of this design is overkill; above it, shard. At 2.5M/s we need ~17 aggregator shards, round up to ~25 for headroom.
116	
117	Storage is a non-issue (live counts under a gig; the tile set is ~30 MB) — *if* we tried to persist the raw firehose it'd be ~2 PB/month, which is exactly why we aggregate-and-drop instead.
118	
119	> 🎙️ Script: "Two numbers matter. One: the firehose is about two and a half million location pings a second at peak — way past what any one machine or partition takes, so I'm sharding ingest by geography. Two: reads are around 800 thousand a second, but every viewer in a city wants the *same* tile, so a CDN absorbs 99% and only about 8 thousand a second hit origin. So I'll spend my budget on swallowing the firehose and on egress, and treat the read path as basically a static-file CDN."
120	
121	---
122	
123	## 4. Core Entities
124	
125	A first-pass scaffold — I'll add full fields in the data model (§7).
126	
127	**LocationPing** — a single "I'm here" event from a driver (or a demand signal from a rider request): who, where (lat/lon), when. It's the raw firehose input and we treat it as disposable.
128	- 🗣️ Analogy: like a single dot a car drops on the map every few seconds — we count the dots, then sweep them away.
129	- ⚠️ Probe: "Do you store every ping?" → No — we fold each ping into a cell counter and drop it; persisting 2.5M/s would be ~2 PB/month for data the next ping replaces in 4 seconds.
130	
131	**Cell** — a geographic bucket (an S2 cell at a chosen zoom level) that owns the *current windowed count* of drivers and demand inside it. This is the unit of aggregation.
132	- 🗣️ Analogy: think of the world as a sheet of graph paper; a Cell is one little square that just tracks "how many cars are in me right now."
133	- ⚠️ Probe: "Some cells are way hotter than others (downtown) — does that hot-spot a shard?" → Yes; the hottest city is ~25K writes/s, so a single hot cell-range is the relief target (sub-sharding + local pre-aggregation).
134	
135	**Tile** — a precomputed, compact snapshot of all the Cells in a city (or viewport) at a zoom level, with a build timestamp. This is what the read path actually serves.
136	- 🗣️ Analogy: a single photo of the city's heat that everyone shares — like a weather radar image refreshed every few seconds.
137	- ⚠️ Probe: "Why a tile and not query cells per request?" → Because it's *shared* — one build per city per 5s serves everyone via CDN, collapsing 800K reads/s into ~200 builds/s.
138	
139	**City / Shard** — the geographic partition that owns a contiguous set of cells, one aggregator owning each. It's how we make ingest and tiling independent and horizontally scalable.
140	- 🗣️ Analogy: like dividing a country into delivery zones so each warehouse only handles its own area — no warehouse needs to talk to another.
141	- ⚠️ Probe: "What's the shard key?" → S2 cell-id prefix (geographic), so nearby cells co-locate and tile builds are local — no cross-shard joins.
142	
143	> 🎙️ Script: "Four things: a LocationPing is the raw dot, a Cell is a little geographic square holding a live count, a Tile is the shared photo of a city we hand everyone, and a City/Shard is the geographic partition that keeps everything independent. The one to watch is the Cell — its uneven heat is what creates hot shards. I'll flesh out the exact fields and stores in the data model."
144	
145	---
146	
147	## 5. API / Interface
148	
149	Default REST over HTTPS; everything under `/v1`. **Versioning:** additive fields (a new optional tile field) ship without a version bump; breaking changes (new tile encoding) get `/v2`. **Pagination:** the only list endpoint (multi-tile viewport) uses a cursor/keyset token over `(zoom, cell_range)` rather than offset/limit — offset re-scans and dupes as cells churn. **Security:** caller identity always comes from the JWT (driver/rider/ops), never a client-supplied id; object-level authz checks the caller may view the requested region (ops dashboards can see any city; a driver app is rate-limited and region-scoped).
150	
151	**FR1 — Ingest a location ping (POST /v1/locations).**
152	- Method: POST — it appends a new event (creates state). Realistically this is *not* a per-request HTTP call; the driver app holds a persistent connection (WebSocket/gRPC stream) and pushes pings, but I'll model the logical contract as POST.
153	- Key fields: `driverId` (from JWT, **not** the body — client can't spoof another driver), `lat`/`lon` (where), `ts` (client time, server clamps it), `available` (bool — only idle drivers count toward supply). Absent on purpose: any cell-id — the server computes the S2 cell, so the client can't forge bucket membership.
154	```
155	// Request (logical; real transport is a persistent stream)
156	POST /v1/locations
157	{ "lat": 40.7411, "lon": -73.9897, "ts": "2026-06-21T18:04:11Z", "available": true }
158	
159	// Response 202
160	{ "accepted": true, "nextPingInMs": 4000 }
161	```
162	- Key decision: returns **202 Accepted**, fire-and-forget — we ack receipt, not durability. Dropping a ping is fine (NFR3); the next one is 4s away. No idempotency key needed because pings are *naturally idempotent by recency* — only the latest ping per driver per window counts, so a duplicate just overwrites itself.
163	
164	**FR3/FR4 — Read a city/viewport heatmap (GET /v1/heatmap).**
165	- Method: GET — pure read, cacheable, no side effects. This is the only verb that makes sense and it's what lets the CDN do its job.
166	- Key fields (query): `city` or `bbox` (the viewport bounding box), `zoom` (controls cell resolution). Absent on purpose: no per-user filter in v1 — the tile is shared (assumption 4).
167	```
168	// Request
169	GET /v1/heatmap?city=nyc&zoom=14
170	
171	// Response 200  (Cache-Control: max-age=5, stale-while-revalidate=10)
172	{
173	  "city": "nyc",
174	  "zoom": 14,
175	  "builtAt": "2026-06-21T18:04:10Z",
176	  "cells": [
177	    { "s2": "89c2594", "supply": 41, "demand": 12 },
178	    { "s2": "89c2597", "supply": 8,  "demand": 33 }
179	  ]
180	}
181	```
182	- Key decision: the response carries `builtAt` and a short `Cache-Control: max-age=5, stale-while-revalidate=10`. That single header is what turns 800K reads/s into ~8K origin misses/s — the **CDN** *(a network of edge servers near users that caches and serves shared content so origin is barely touched)* serves the same tile to everyone for 5 seconds, then revalidates in the background while still serving the slightly-stale copy.
183	
184	> 🎙️ Script: "Two endpoints carry the whole system. Drivers push location pings — I model it as POST but it's really a persistent stream — and I just 202 them, because losing one ping doesn't matter, the next is four seconds out. Reads are a plain GET for a city at a zoom level, and the magic is one cache header: max-age five seconds with stale-while-revalidate, so the CDN hands everyone the same tile and origin barely notices. Identity always comes from the JWT, and the server computes the cell itself so nobody can forge which bucket they're in."
185	
186	---
187	
188	## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time)
189	
190	Shared color language across every diagram: blue = clients, green = our services, amber = streams/queues, grey = datastores, purple = external (CDN). Same component keeps the same name and color everywhere.
191	
192	### 6.1 "Ingest the location firehose" (FR1)
193	
194	This is the write side: swallow ~2.5M pings/s without dropping the floor out, and do it cheaply since the data is disposable.
195	
196	**Architecture decisions table**
197	
198	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
199	|---|---|---|---|---|
200	| Edge gateway | A thin front door that terminates the driver's persistent connection and forwards pings | Drivers hold long-lived connections; a stream gateway is far cheaper than a new TCP+TLS handshake every 4s | Not per-ping HTTPS (handshake cost dominates at 2.5M/s); not a load balancer alone (no connection state) | Stateful connections to manage; needs graceful drain on deploy |
201	| Ingest stream | A durable, partitioned log that buffers the firehose so consumers can fall behind without loss | Decouples spiky 2.5M/s ingest from aggregators; partition by cell-prefix gives geographic locality | Not direct-to-Redis (a slow aggregator would back-pressure the gateway); not a DB (no DB takes 2.5M writes/s) | A few seconds of buffering latency; we tune retention short (minutes) |
202	| Aggregator workers | Stateless-ish consumers that fold pings into per-cell windowed counters in memory | Counting is just increment-in-a-time-bucket; keeping it in RAM is the only way to hit firehose rate | Not a stream-SQL engine for v1 (operational weight); not per-ping DB upsert (melts the DB) | In-memory state is lost on crash — but it rebuilds from the stream in seconds |
203	
204	> 🎙️ Narration: "The driver app keeps one connection open and dribbles pings through an edge gateway — way cheaper than reconnecting every four seconds. The gateway drops each ping onto a partitioned log, which I'm using like a shock absorber: it's partitioned by geography so a New York ping and a London ping land on different partitions and never contend. Then a fleet of aggregator workers reads its own partitions and just increments an in-memory counter per cell per time-bucket. I deliberately did *not* write pings to a database — nothing relational takes two and a half million writes a second — and the counters live in RAM because that's the only way to keep up; if a worker dies, it rebuilds from the log in seconds."
205	
206	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
207	> **Partitioned log (Kafka-style stream)** — a durable, append-only queue split into independent partitions so many consumers read in parallel. Here it buffers the 2.5M/s firehose and shards it by geography.
208	> **Partition by cell-prefix** — choosing the partition from the S2 cell id's leading bits, so geographically-near pings co-locate. Here it means one city's pings all land together, enabling local tile builds.
209	> **Windowed counter** — a count scoped to the last N seconds (e.g. 4 one-second buckets summed), not an all-time total. Here it's how we get "drivers *right now*" instead of "drivers ever."
210	> **Back-pressure** — when a slow consumer signals upstream to slow down rather than overflow. Here the log absorbs it so the gateway never has to block drivers.
211

231	**Numbered step narration**
232	1. **Push location ping.** The driver app sends its current position over its open connection. *Why the gateway:* it owns connection state and terminates TLS once, not per-ping. *State change:* a ping is now in flight. *What could go wrong:* connection drops — fine, the app reconnects and the gap is 4s of missing dots, invisible at city zoom.
233	2. **Append by cell-prefix.** The gateway computes the S2 cell, picks the partition from its prefix, and appends. *Why the log:* it absorbs bursts so a slow aggregator can't back up onto the gateway. *State change:* the ping is durably buffered for a few minutes. *What could go wrong:* a partition is hot (downtown) — we sub-shard that prefix range across more partitions.
234	3. **Consume own partitions.** Each aggregator reads only its assigned partitions. *Why:* geographic locality means a worker owns whole cities, so tile builds are local. *State change:* worker advances its offset. *What could go wrong:* worker lag spikes — we alarm on consumer lag and add workers.
235	4. **Increment cell counter.** The worker folds the ping into the current time-bucket for that cell. *Why in memory:* 2.5M increments/s is RAM work, not disk. *State change:* the cell's windowed count goes up by one. *What could go wrong:* worker crash loses in-RAM counts — rebuilt by replaying the last window from the log.
236	
237	> 💬 Say-while-drawing: "The log is a shock absorber — it lets the writes be huge and bursty while the counters stay calm, and partitioning by geography means no two cities ever fight for the same counter."
238	
239	> 🎙️ Script: "On the write side, drivers stream pings through a gateway into a partitioned log that I partition by geography. Aggregator workers each own a slice of the world and just increment in-memory counters per cell per time window. The big call is that I never persist a raw ping — they're disposable, the next one's four seconds away — so the whole write path is a buffer plus a counter, which is the only thing that survives two and a half million events a second."

241	### 6.2 "Geo-bucket and aggregate into cell density" (FR2)
242	
243	This slice is the *logic* inside the aggregator: turning a stream of (lat, lon) into windowed per-cell counts at the right resolution. It mostly lives on the components 6.1 already drew, so I'll highlight only the new state it adds.
244	
245	**Architecture decisions table**
246	
247	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
248	|---|---|---|---|---|
249	| S2 cell indexing | A library that turns any lat/lon into a hierarchical cell id at any zoom | S2 gives stable, hierarchical buckets so a finer cell rolls up to a coarser one for free across zoom levels | Not a flat lat/lon grid (distorts near poles, no hierarchy); not geohash (less uniform cell area) | A small CPU cost per ping to compute the cell id |
250	| Sliding time window | Several 1-second sub-buckets summed to give "last N seconds" | A driver that went offline must decay out within seconds, not linger forever | Not an all-time counter (never decays — a parked car counts at midnight); not exact event-time (overkill) | Slight under/over-count at bucket edges — acceptable for display |
251	| Cell state store (Redis) | An in-memory key-value store holding the live count per cell | Sub-millisecond increments at firehose rate; under 1 GB total so it fits comfortably | Not Postgres (can't take the write rate); not local-only RAM (lost entirely on crash with no shared view) | Eventual durability — it's a cache of truth-from-the-stream, rebuildable |
252	
253	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
254	> **S2 geometry** — Google's system for mapping the sphere onto a quadtree of cells, so every location has a cell id at every zoom level. Here it's our bucket key and our zoom mechanism.
255	> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store with atomic increments. Here it holds the live per-cell counts so reads and tile builds are instant.
256	> **Hierarchical roll-up** — coarser zoom = drop bits off the cell id and sum children. Here it lets one fine-grained count serve multiple zoom levels.
257

263	    subgraph Bucket["Bucket and window"]

265	        S2 -->|"3 pick time bucket"| WIN[Window manager]
266	    end
267	    WIN -->|"4 atomic incr cell bucket"| RC[(Cell state in memory)]
268	    WIN -.->|"5 expire old buckets"| RC

277	
278	**Numbered step narration**
279	1. **Read ping.** Worker pulls the next ping. *Why:* same consumer as 6.1. *State:* offset advances. *What if:* duplicate ping — harmless, recency-idempotent.
280	2. **lat/lon → S2 cell.** Compute the cell id at the finest stored zoom (e.g. level 14). *Why S2:* hierarchical, uniform-ish cells. *State:* ping now has a bucket key. *What if:* point on a cell boundary — deterministic tie-break, irrelevant at scale.
281	3. **Pick time bucket.** Choose the current 1-second sub-bucket. *Why:* lets us sum the last N for a sliding window and expire the oldest. *State:* (cell, second) chosen. *What if:* clock skew — clamp ping `ts` to server time.
282	4. **Atomic increment.** Increment `(cell, available, second)` in Redis. *Why atomic:* many workers may touch overlapping cities on rebalance. *State:* count +1. *What if:* hot cell contention — see §8 deep dive (local pre-aggregation).
283	5. **Expire old buckets.** Buckets older than the window TTL out. *Why:* offline drivers must decay. *State:* stale counts vanish. *What if:* TTL too long — parked cars inflate density; tuned to ~10s.
284	
285	> 💬 Say-while-drawing: "The window is what makes it *live* — a car that goes quiet just falls out of the count in a few seconds because its old buckets expire."
286	
287	> 🎙️ Script: "Inside the aggregator, every ping becomes an S2 cell id — that's our bucket and our zoom dial in one. I keep counts in short one-second sub-buckets and sum the last several, so 'how many cars are here right now' is real-time and a driver who goes offline decays out automatically. It all lives in Redis with atomic increments, which is fast enough for the firehose and small enough — under a gig — to keep entirely in memory."

289	### 6.3 "Build & serve precomputed heatmap tiles" (FR3) + 6.4 "Read by viewport/zoom" (FR4)
290	
291	These two are one mechanism: a tile builder snapshots cell state into compact per-city tiles on a cadence, and the read path serves those tiles through a CDN. I'll diagram them together.
292	
293	**Architecture decisions table**
294	
295	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
296	|---|---|---|---|---|
297	| Tile builder | A worker that, every 5s, reads a city's cells and serializes a compact tile | Decouples build (200/s, cheap) from reads (800K/s) so reads never touch Redis | Not build-on-read (every read hits Redis = melts it at 800K/s); not push-per-cell (chatty) | Tile is up to 5s stale — fine per NFR2 |
298	| Tile store | A small object/KV store holding the current tile per city per zoom | Origin needs a stable, CDN-fillable artifact; ~30 MB total | Not serving straight from Redis (couples read load to write store); not a big DB (overkill) | One extra hop on a cache miss — only ~8K/s see it |
299	| CDN | Edge servers that cache the tile near users and serve it for its TTL | Collapses 800K reads/s to ~8K origin misses/s; tiles are identical for all viewers | Not origin-only (800K/s origin = huge fleet + cost); not client P2P (complexity, trust) | Up to 5s of edge staleness; cold-cache stampede risk (handled in §8) |
300	
301	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
302	> **Tile builder** — a periodic job that snapshots live counts into a single compact file per city. Here it turns 800K reads into 200 builds.
303	> **stale-while-revalidate** — a cache directive that serves a slightly-stale copy while fetching a fresh one in the background. Here it kills the thundering-herd at TTL expiry.
304	> **Object/KV tile store** — a small store the CDN fills from. Here it's the origin of truth for "current tile."
305

328	**Numbered step narration**
329	1. **GET heatmap.** Viewer asks for a city tile at a zoom. *Why CDN first:* it's the cheapest possible hop. *State:* request hits the nearest edge. *What if:* unknown city — origin returns an empty tile, not an error.
330	2. **Miss fill.** On a cache miss the edge fetches the current tile from the tile store. *Why:* only misses (~8K/s) reach origin. *State:* edge now caches the tile for its TTL. *What if:* simultaneous misses across many edges — bounded by city count, not viewer count.
331	3. **Cached hit served.** For the next 5s every viewer in that city gets the cached tile directly. *Why:* shared tile = ~99% hit. *State:* origin untouched. *What if:* TTL expiry stampede — stale-while-revalidate serves the old copy meanwhile (deep dive §8).
332	4. **Read cell counts.** Independently, the tile builder reads a city's live cells every 5s. *Why async:* build cost (200/s) is fully decoupled from reads. *State:* builder holds a fresh snapshot. *What if:* Redis briefly unavailable — builder reuses the last tile, freshness degrades gracefully.
333	5. **Write current tile.** Builder serializes a compact tile and overwrites the city's current tile. *Why overwrite:* only "now" matters; no history. *State:* tile store has the fresh tile. *What if:* builder lags — `builtAt` exposes staleness to the client.
334	
335	> 💬 Say-while-drawing: "Reads and builds never touch each other — one path serves a frozen photo through the CDN, the other quietly takes a new photo every five seconds."
336	
337	> 🎙️ Script: "The read path is almost embarrassingly simple: a tile builder snapshots each city's counts into a tiny file every five seconds, and the CDN serves that same file to everyone for five seconds. So eight hundred thousand reads a second collapse into about eight thousand origin misses and two hundred builds — the read store never sees the read traffic. That decoupling is the whole trick; it's why a map this big costs almost nothing per view."

372	## 7. Data Model & Storage
373	
374	**PART A — Entity table**
375	
376	| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency |
377	|---|---|---|---|---|
378	| LocationPing | `driverId`, `lat`, `lon`, `ts`, `available` (no PK — ephemeral stream record) | Ingest stream log | **S2 cell-prefix** | At-least-once delivery, lossy-tolerant |
379	| Cell (live count) | **`(s2CellId, secondBucket, available)` = PK**; value = count | Redis (in-memory) | **s2CellId prefix** | Eventual; rebuildable from stream |
380	| Tile | **`(cityId, zoom)` = PK**; value = compact blob + `builtAt` | Tile store (object/KV) | **cityId** | Eventual, ≤5s stale by design |
381	| City / Shard | **`cityId` = PK**; `bbox`, `s2Range`, `ownerShard` | Config DB (Postgres) | none (small, replicated) | Strong (rarely changes) |
382	
383	**PART B — Storage decision cards**
384	
385	**🗄️ Ingest stream log (Kafka / Kinesis / Pub-Sub) — used for: LocationPing firehose**
386	1. What it is (plain words): a durable, append-only queue split into independent partitions, so producers write fast and many consumers read in parallel without stepping on each other.
387	2. Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it: we have ~2.5M tiny writes/s that must be *buffered* so a slow aggregator can fall behind without dropping pings, and *partitioned by geography* so cities are independent. A log is the only thing that gives both at this rate.
388	3. What we considered and rejected:
389	   - **Write straight to Redis/DB** — a slow consumer would back-pressure all the way up to the driver connection; the log decouples that.
390	   - **A managed queue (SQS)** — great for work items, but no partition-ordering or replay and per-message cost is brutal at 2.5M/s.
391	   - **A time-series DB ingest** — can't take 2.5M writes/s without a buffer in front anyway.
392	4. Trade-off we accept: a few seconds of buffering latency, and we keep retention short (minutes) since pings are disposable.
393	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I put a partitioned log in front of everything as a shock absorber. It's partitioned by geography so New York and London never contend, and because pings are disposable I keep retention to a few minutes — it's a buffer, not a database."
394	
395	**🗄️ Redis (in-memory KV) — used for: Cell live counts**
396	1. What it is (plain words): a key-value store that lives entirely in RAM with atomic increment operations, so reads and writes are sub-millisecond.
397	2. Why we picked it: counting at firehose rate means atomic increments many times per cell per second — only an in-memory store does that, and the whole state is under 1 GB so it fits easily.
398	3. What we considered and rejected:
399	   - **Postgres** — can't take the write rate; row locks on hot cells would serialize downtown.
400	   - **Cassandra/Dynamo** — durable but write-amplified and overkill for data the window expires in seconds.
401	   - **Pure local process RAM** — fastest, but lost entirely on crash with no shared view for the tile builder.
402	4. Trade-off we accept: eventual durability — Redis is a cache of truth-that-lives-in-the-stream, rebuildable in seconds by replaying the last window.
403	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Live counts go in Redis because I need atomic increments at firehose speed, and it's tiny — under a gig — so it all sits in memory. It's not my source of truth; the stream is. If Redis dies I replay the last ten seconds and I'm whole again."
404	
405	**🗄️ Tile store (object store / KV like S3 + edge) — used for: precomputed Tiles**
406	1. What it is (plain words): a simple store holding one small compact file per city per zoom — the "current photo" the CDN fills from.
407	2. Why we picked it: the read path needs a stable, cacheable artifact the CDN can pull on a miss; ~30 MB total and overwritten in place.
408	3. What we considered and rejected:
409	   - **Serve directly from Redis** — couples 800K reads/s of pressure onto the write store; the builder breaks that coupling.
410	   - **A relational DB** — no reason to pay for transactions on an overwrite-only blob.
411	4. Trade-off we accept: one extra hop on a cache miss, but only ~8K/s ever see it.
412	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Tiles live in a dead-simple object store the CDN fills from. It's basically a folder of one small file per city that I overwrite every five seconds — no history, no transactions, nothing fancy."
413	
414	**PART C — Per-operation consistency**
415	
416	| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right |
417	|---|---|---|---|
418	| Ingest a ping | Stream log | At-least-once, lossy-OK | A lost ping is replaced in 4s; durability would cost 10x for no product value |
419	| Increment cell count | Redis | Eventual, atomic per-key | Display heatmap tolerates a few seconds skew; atomicity prevents lost increments |
420	| Build a tile | Redis → tile store | Snapshot (point-in-time) | Tile is a consistent photo at `builtAt`; staleness exposed to client |
421	| Read a tile | CDN → tile store | Eventual, ≤5s stale | The product *is* "a few seconds old"; freshness traded for ~free reads |
422	| Read-your-own-writes | — | N/A by design | A driver does NOT need to see their own dot appear instantly; aggregate display, not personal state |
423	
424	**PART D — Data lifecycle (largest/fastest-growing data: the ping firehose)**
425	
426	The firehose is by far the biggest flow — if persisted it'd be ~2 PB/month (§3). The lifecycle answer is **don't persist raw pings at all**: they're folded into windowed counters and the stream itself uses **short time-partitioned retention** (~5–15 minutes), reclaimed by **dropping whole expired partitions** (an O(1) segment delete), never row-by-row deletes. If product later needs historical replay/analytics, we'd fork a *sampled* copy (e.g. 1%) to a columnar warehouse (BigQuery/S3+Parquet) on a 30–90 day retention — that's a separate, opt-in pipeline, not the hot path. Redis cell state self-cleans via per-bucket TTL.
427	
428	> 🎙️ Script: "The core stores are deliberately humble. A partitioned log buffers the firehose, Redis holds the live counts because I need atomic increments at memory speed, and a tiny object store holds the tiles the CDN serves. I considered persisting the raw pings to a time-series DB and rejected it — it'd be nearly two petabytes a month for data the next ping overwrites, so I aggregate and drop, keeping the stream retention to minutes and reclaiming space by dropping whole partitions, never deleting rows."

432	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
433	
434	> 🆘 If you get stuck: go back to the two numbers — 2.5M pings/s in, 800K reads/s out — and ask "what's the binding bottleneck and what relieves it?" The method is the safety net.
435	
436	### How do we swallow a 2.5M pings/s firehose without melting?
437	
438	**#### Bad: Write every ping straight to a database**
439	
440	🗣️ What this is in plain words: every time a car says "I'm here," we write a row to a database and run a count query when someone asks.
441	
442	Approach: driver POST → app server → `INSERT` into a locations table → `SELECT count(*) ... GROUP BY cell` on read.
443	
444	Why people try this: it's the obvious first instinct — "it's just data, put it in the DB."
445	
446	⚠️ What breaks: at 2.5M writes/s, no single relational DB comes close (a beefy Postgres tops out maybe ~10–50K writes/s); you'd shard into hundreds of DBs purely for ingest, and the read-time `GROUP BY` re-scans millions of rows per request. The connection pool (~100–200 connections) exhausts in milliseconds.
447	
448	🔁 What forces the upgrade: the write rate alone — 2.5M/s is ~50–250x a single DB's ceiling.
449	
450	**#### Good: Buffer in a partitioned log, aggregate in memory**
451	
452	↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: Bad coupled durable per-row writes to firehose rate. Good removes the DB from the hot path entirely — pings go to a buffer, and counting happens in RAM.
453	
454	🗣️ What this is in plain words: we drop every ping into a fast queue, and a worker keeps a running tally in memory instead of writing each one down.
455	
456	Approach: gateway → **partitioned log** (partition by S2 cell-prefix) → aggregator workers do atomic in-memory increments per `(cell, second)` in Redis → tile builder snapshots.
457	
458	Why this seemed reasonable / better: the log absorbs bursts and the increment is O(1) in RAM, so we ride the firehose.
459	
460	⚠️ What breaks: one partition per *region* still concentrates a hot city — downtown's ~25K pings/s plus boundary skew can hot-spot a single partition and a single aggregator, and a worker crash loses its in-RAM counts (rebuildable, but a blip).
461	
462	🔁 What forces the upgrade: a single dense city or a single dense *cell* (a stadium letting out) overloads one partition/worker — uniform sharding doesn't imply uniform traffic.
463	
464	**Good — mechanism diagram**

468	    GW -->|"by cell prefix"| P1[[Partition NA]]:::queue
469	    GW -->|"by cell prefix"| P2[[Partition EU]]:::queue
470	    P1 -->|"consume"| A1[Aggregator 1]:::svc
471	    P2 -->|"consume"| A2[Aggregator 2]:::svc
472	    A1 -->|"atomic incr"| RC[(Cell counts)]:::db
473	    A2 -->|"atomic incr"| RC
474	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
475	    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
476	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
477	```
478	
479	**#### Great: Sub-shard hot ranges + local pre-aggregation + rebuildable state**
480	
481	↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: Good used coarse geographic partitions, so a single dense city overwhelmed one worker. Great splits hot ranges finer and pre-aggregates at the edge so the central counter sees far fewer writes.
482	
483	🗣️ What this is in plain words: for crowded areas we use more, smaller buckets *and* we let the gateway add up nearby pings before sending, so the busy parts of the city get more help and the central tally does less work.
484	
485	Approach: (1) **dynamic sub-sharding** — a hot cell-prefix is split across multiple partitions keyed by `(cellPrefix, hash(driverId) % k)` so downtown spreads over `k` partitions/workers; (2) **local pre-aggregation** — each gateway/worker batches one second of pings per cell and emits a single `+N` instead of N `+1`s, cutting central increments by the drivers-per-cell factor; (3) **rebuildable state** — Redis counts are reconstructable by replaying the last window from the log, so a crash costs seconds, not data.
486	
487	🔢 Decision-forcing math: 2.5M/s ÷ ~150K/s per aggregator = ~17 shards; round to ~25 for headroom. Local pre-aggregation: in a dense cell with ~40 drivers, 40 `+1`s become one `+40` — a ~40x reduction in central write ops on exactly the hottest cells, so the hot cell stops being the bottleneck.
488	
489	✅ Failure matrix
490	| Scenario | What happens |
491	|---|---|
492	| Aggregator crashes | Partition reassigned; new owner replays last ~10s window from log; counts whole in seconds |
493	| Hot cell (stadium out) | Sub-shard split across `k` partitions + local pre-agg collapses N pings to one +N |
494	| Log partition lag | Tile `builtAt` shows staleness; alarm on consumer lag; add workers |
495	| Duplicate ping | Recency-idempotent — only latest window matters; double-count decays out in seconds |
496	| Whole-AZ loss | Aggregators are stateless+rebuildable; respawn in healthy AZ, replay from log |
497	
498	> 🎙️ Script: "The firehose is the hard part. I buffer it in a log partitioned by geography so cities are independent, and aggregators keep counts in memory. The senior move is admitting geography isn't uniform — downtown is a hot shard — so I sub-shard hot ranges across several workers and I pre-aggregate at the edge, turning forty separate plus-ones into a single plus-forty. That collapses the hottest cell by roughly forty times, and because the counts are just a replayable view of the log, a crashed worker rebuilds in seconds."
499	
500	> 🧠 If they ask "why not exactly-once?": "The product is approximate and the window expires in seconds, so a dropped or doubled ping is invisible. Paying for exactly-once here is buying a guarantee the heatmap can't even perceive."
501	
502	### How do we serve 800K reads/s cheaply without a thundering herd?
503	
504	**#### Bad: Build the tile on every read**
505	
506	🗣️ Plain words: every time someone opens the map, we go ask Redis for all the counts and assemble a fresh tile just for them.
507	
508	Approach: GET → service → read all city cells from Redis → serialize → return.
509	
510	Why people try this: it's the simplest correct thing and feels "always fresh."
511	
512	⚠️ What breaks: 800K reads/s each doing a multi-key Redis read melts Redis (a node tops ~150K ops/s, and each read is many keys) and burns CPU re-serializing identical tiles millions of times. We're recomputing the same answer for everyone.
513	
514	🔁 What forces the upgrade: the read rate is 100%+ redundant — everyone in a city wants the identical tile.
515	
516	**#### Good: Precompute tiles + CDN with short TTL**
517	
518	↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: Bad recomputed a shared answer per request. Good computes it once per city per interval and lets a cache fan it out.
519	
520	🗣️ Plain words: we build the photo once every five seconds and put it on a cache near users that hands out copies.
521	
522	Approach: tile builder writes one tile per city to the tile store; CDN serves with `max-age=5`. Reads collapse to ~8K origin misses/s + 200 builds/s.
523	
524	⚠️ What breaks: when a TTL expires, every edge for a hot city misses *at the same instant* and stampedes the origin — a synchronized "thundering herd" 5-second pulse.
525	
526	🔁 What forces the upgrade: synchronized expiry turns a smooth ~8K/s into spiky origin pulses.
527	
528	**Good — mechanism diagram**
529	```mermaid
530	flowchart TD
531	    V[Viewers]:::client
532	    V -->|"GET tile"| CDN[CDN edge]:::ext
533	    CDN -->|"hit served"| V
534	    CDN -.->|"on TTL miss"| TS[(Tile store)]:::db
535	    BUILD[Tile builder]:::svc -->|"every 5s overwrite"| TS
536	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
537	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
538	    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
539	    classDef ext fill:#3a2a5c,stroke:#9b6bf0,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
540	```
541	
542	**#### Great: stale-while-revalidate + single-flight + jittered TTL**
543	
544	↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: Good let TTLs expire in lockstep, stampeding origin. Great never makes a viewer wait on a refresh and collapses concurrent misses to one origin fetch.
545	
546	🗣️ Plain words: when the cached photo gets old, we keep handing out the slightly-old one while a *single* background fetch grabs the new one, and we stagger when copies expire so they don't all refresh at once.
547	
548	Approach: (1) **stale-while-revalidate** — serve the stale tile instantly, refresh in the background; viewers never block. (2) **single-flight (request coalescing)** — the edge sends exactly one origin fetch per tile even if 10K viewers miss simultaneously. (3) **jittered TTL** — add ±1s random jitter so cities don't expire in lockstep. (4) push-on-build option: builder can proactively warm CDN for the hottest cities.
549	
550	🔢 Decision-forcing math: without single-flight, a hot city with 1,000 edges all missing at once = 1,000 simultaneous origin fetches per expiry × hot cities = origin spike. With single-flight + SWR, origin sees ~1 fetch per tile per TTL = ~200/s steady, never a pulse.
551	
552	✅ Failure matrix
553	| Scenario | What happens |
554	|---|---|
555	| TTL expiry herd | SWR serves stale; single-flight sends 1 origin fetch; no spike |
556	| Tile store down | CDN serves last good tile; freshness degrades; alarm fires |
557	| Builder lags | `builtAt` exposes staleness; client can gray out if too old |
558	| Unknown city | Origin returns empty tile (200), not 404 — map just shows no heat |
559	| Cold cache (deploy/flush) | Worst case ~800K/s to origin briefly — mitigated by pre-warming hottest cities + single-flight |
560	
561	> 🎙️ Script: "On reads the win is precompute-plus-CDN: one tile per city per five seconds, fanned out at the edge, so eight hundred thousand reads become about eight thousand origin misses. The trap is synchronized cache expiry stampeding origin, so I serve the slightly-stale tile while a single background fetch refreshes it, and I jitter the TTLs so cities don't all expire together. Origin then sees a flat couple-hundred fetches a second instead of pulses."
562	
563	> 🧠 If they ask "what about cold cache after a flush?": "Worst case every viewer misses at once — ~800K/s. I pre-warm the top cities on deploy and rely on single-flight so even a cold city only generates one origin fetch, not thousands."
564	
565	### How do we keep the heatmap *fresh* yet cheap (update frequency vs cost)?
566	
567	🗣️ Plain words: refreshing more often makes the map livelier but costs more builds and egress — we have to pick the cheapest cadence that still feels live.
568	
569	Approach: cadence is a dial. Build/serve every 5s → 200 builds/s, ~$1.2M/mo egress. Drop to 2s → 2.5x the builds *and* up to 2.5x egress (more frequent re-fetches) for marginal perceived freshness; go to 10s → halve egress but blobs feel laggy when demand shifts fast.
570	
571	🔢 Decision-forcing math: egress (§3) scales with refresh frequency — at 30 KB/tile and ~800K effective fetches/s, halving the interval roughly doubles the dominant cost line. The lever that beats cadence: **shrink the tile** (quantize counts, delta-encode vs last tile, gzip) — a 3x smaller tile cuts the $1.2M/mo egress directly without touching freshness.
572	
573	⚠️ What breaks at the extremes: too fast = cost explosion for freshness users can't perceive; too slow = drivers chase emptied blobs (NFR2 violated).
574	
575	> 🎙️ Script: "Freshness is a cost dial, not a free knob. Every time I refresh faster I pay in builds and especially egress, which is my biggest line item. So I hold the cadence at about five seconds — the point where it still feels live — and I attack cost by shrinking the tile with delta-encoding and quantization instead, which cuts egress without making the map feel slower."
576	
577	> 🧠 If they ask "why 5 seconds, not 1?": "A driver can't react to a one-second change anyway, and one-second refresh roughly 5x's my egress bill for freshness nobody perceives — five seconds is the knee of the curve."
578	
579	---
580	
581	## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost
582	
583	**9A — Availability targets.** Read path target **99.95%** (~4.4 hours/year down), ingest **99.9%** (~8.7 hours/year). Mechanisms: the read path is a CDN in front of a tiny object store — both are independently highly available, and the CDN serves last-good tiles even if origin is down. **Multi-AZ** *(multiple data centres in one region, so if one building loses power the others take over automatically)* for aggregators, log, and Redis. Aggregators are stateless+rebuildable, so AZ loss is a respawn-and-replay, not an outage. Ingest gets the looser target because a brief gap self-heals on the next ping.
584	
585	**9B — Per-dependency failure table**
586	| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what the USER SEES) | Recovery |
587	|---|---|---|---|
588	| CDN edge | Reads can't be served at edge | Map briefly retries; client shows last tile it had | Failover to alt CDN / serve from origin |
589	| Tile store | No new tiles to fill cache | CDN serves last-good tile; heat slowly goes stale | Restore from builder re-write; ~minutes |
590	| Tile builder | Tiles stop refreshing | Heat freezes at last `builtAt`; client can gray out if too old | Respawn worker; resumes from Redis |
591	| Redis (cell state) | No live counts | Builder reuses last tile; freshness degrades, no crash | Rebuild from log replay in seconds |
592	| Ingest log | Pings can't be buffered | Counts stop updating; tiles freeze | Failover partitions; gateway retries briefly |
593	| Edge gateway | Drivers can't push pings | Counts decay toward stale; heat fades | Drivers reconnect to healthy gateway |
594	
595	**9C — RPO / RTO.** Plain words first: **RPO** (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing in a crash; **RTO** (Recovery Time Objective) = how fast we're back.
596	| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
597	|---|---|---|---|
598	| Live cell counts | ~10s (the window) | seconds | Replay last window from log; counts are derived, not source |
599	| Tiles | ~5s (one build) | seconds | Re-built from Redis on next cadence |
600	| Ping firehose | seconds, lossy-OK | seconds | Short-retention log; no durable raw storage by design |
601	| City/shard config | ~0 | minutes | Postgres, replicated + backed up |
602	
603	Notice RPO is *intentionally* loose for everything but config — the product is approximate, so we don't pay for RPO≈0 on disposable data.
604	
605	**9D — Cost.** Top 3 drivers: (1) **CDN/tile egress ~$1.2M/mo** — every tile byte served to every viewer, the dominant line; (2) **ingest stream + aggregator compute** — ~25 aggregator shards plus the log fleet for the 2.5M/s firehose; (3) **Redis** — small (under 1 GB) so cheap, mostly the HA replicas. Order of magnitude: low single-digit millions/month, egress-dominated. **Biggest optimization:** shrink the tile (delta-encode + quantize + gzip) — a 3x reduction cuts the top line ~$1.2M → ~$0.4M/mo. Unit economics: **~$0.60 per 1M tile reads** today, ~$0.20 after tile compression.
606	
607	**Back-pressure (hottest async path = ingest log → aggregators):** the log is a bounded buffer; under overload aggregators shed by **dropping the oldest pings first** (lossy-OK by NFR3) rather than blocking the gateway — we protect freshness of *recent* data over completeness of old data. Trigger signal: consumer lag and queue depth; alarm at lag > 10s (one window).
608	
609	**Observability:** golden signals — latency (read p99, build time), traffic (ingest QPS, read QPS), errors (gateway 5xx, build failures), saturation (consumer lag, Redis ops/s). A **trace-id** is stamped at the gateway and propagated through the log → aggregator → builder so one driver's ping is traceable across the async hop. Burn-rate alert on the read-path SLO at 2% budget/hour.
610	
611	**Rate-limiting:** the public read endpoint uses a token-bucket per client/IP; over-limit returns **HTTP 429 with Retry-After**. The limiter state is itself a global coordinator (a shared Redis counter) — kept regional to avoid a cross-region hop.
612	
613	> 🎙️ Script: "When things fail, the user mostly sees *staleness*, not errors — the heat freezes or fades rather than going blank, because the CDN keeps serving the last good tile and counts rebuild from the log in seconds. The single most important reliability fact is that nothing on the hot path is a source of truth: cell counts and tiles are both derived, so recovery is replay-and-rebuild. Cost-wise it's an egress story — millions a month in tile bytes — so the one lever I'd pull is compressing the tile."
614	
615	---
616	
617	## 10. Trade-off Ledger
618	
619	**🔀 Decision: Aggregate-and-drop vs persist the raw firehose**
620	1. What we chose and why: never store raw pings — fold into windowed counters and drop. The data is disposable and the next ping arrives in 4s.
621	2. What we gave up: no historical replay or exact audit from the hot path; we'd need a separate sampled pipeline for analytics.
622	3. 🗣️ Plain words: like counting people walking through a door with a clicker instead of photographing every person — you get the count, you don't keep the photos.
623	4. When this reverses: if regulators require exact, auditable driver-density history, or product wants time-travel replay → add a durable, sampled event log to a warehouse.
624	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I aggregate and drop because keeping the firehose would be nearly two petabytes a month for data that expires in seconds — if we ever need history I'll fork a sampled copy to a warehouse separately."
625	
626	**🔀 Decision: Precomputed shared tiles + CDN vs query-per-request**
627	1. What we chose and why: build one tile per city per interval and fan it out; reads become ~free and origin barely sees traffic.
628	2. What we gave up: per-user personalization and sub-5s freshness — everyone gets the same slightly-stale tile.
629	3. 🗣️ Plain words: one shared weather-radar image instead of a custom map drawn for each person.
630	4. When this reverses: if heatmaps must be filtered per driver (vehicle class, service line) → the tile stops being shared and CDN caching collapses; I'd precompute a small set of variants or push filtering to the client.
631	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "The whole read economy depends on everyone sharing one tile — that's what makes 800K reads cost almost nothing. Personalization is the thing that would break it."
632	
633	**🔀 Decision: Per-city geographic sharding vs a global aggregation tier**
634	1. What we chose and why: shard by S2 cell-prefix so each city is independent; no coordinator, linear scaling.
635	2. What we gave up: a live global zoom-out view requires a separate roll-up tier we don't build by default.
636	3. 🗣️ Plain words: independent regional warehouses that never phone each other, versus one central HQ that knows everything.
637	4. When this reverses: if a worldwide live density map becomes a hard requirement → add hierarchical city→region→world roll-ups, reintroducing a (read-only) aggregation coordinator.
638	5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Geography is my shard key, so every city stands alone and there's no global bottleneck — the only thing that brings a coordinator back is demanding a live global view."
639	
640	**One-line CAP/PACELC:** the core path is **AP** — under a partition we keep serving (possibly stale) tiles rather than blocking; and under PACELC, in normal operation we favor **latency (and cost) over consistency**, because a few seconds of staleness is the product, not a defect.
641	
642	> 🎙️ Script: "My three least-certain calls are aggregate-and-drop, shared tiles, and per-city sharding — and they all trace to one principle: this is an approximate, shared, display artifact, so I trade exactness and personalization for throughput and near-free reads. The moment any of those product assumptions flip — exact counts, per-driver filtering, or a live global view — I'd revisit the matching decision, but until then this is the cheapest design that meets the SLOs."
643	
644	---
645	
646	## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers
647	
648	**❓ "Two drivers' pings race into the same cell at the same millisecond — do we lose a count?"**
649	The mechanism: increments go to Redis with an atomic INCR per `(cell, second, available)` key, so concurrent updates serialize at the key and neither is lost. With local pre-aggregation, a worker batches a second of pings per cell into one `+N`, so contention drops further.
650	🗣️ In plain words: the counter is add-safe, so two cars landing at once both get counted.
651	💬 One-liner: "Atomic increments plus per-second batching mean concurrent pings can't clobber each other."
652	
653	**❓ "What happens when the payment—sorry, when Redis crashes?"**
654	The mechanism: Redis holds *derived* counts, not source of truth. On crash, aggregators replay the last ~10-second window from the ingest log and rebuild every cell count in seconds; meanwhile the tile builder serves the last good tile so the map just freezes briefly.
655	🗣️ In plain words: the live counts are rebuildable from the recent stream, so a Redis crash is a few seconds of stale heat, not data loss.
656	💬 One-liner: "Redis is a cache of the stream — we replay the last window and we're whole."
657	
658	**❓ "How do you handle a flash crowd — a stadium emptying into one cell?"**
659	The mechanism: that's a hot cell (~25K/s city, even denser locally). We sub-shard the hot cell-prefix across `k` partitions/workers and pre-aggregate at the edge, collapsing N `+1`s into one `+N` (~40x on dense cells), so the hottest cell stops being the bottleneck.
660	🗣️ In plain words: for crowded spots we split the work across more helpers and add the pings up locally before reporting.
661	💬 One-liner: "Sub-shard the hot range and pre-aggregate — the busiest cell does the least central work."
662	
663	**❓ "Can the same driver be double-counted?"**
664	The mechanism: counts are windowed and recency-based — only the latest ping per driver per window matters, and old buckets expire. A duplicate ping just re-overwrites the same window and decays out in seconds.
665	🗣️ In plain words: a stray duplicate ping can't inflate things for long because the window forgets it almost immediately.
666	💬 One-liner: "Recency-idempotent windows mean duplicates self-correct in seconds."
667	
668	**❓ "How do you stop a viewer from forging a region they shouldn't see, or spoofing pings as another driver?"**
669	The mechanism: caller identity comes from the JWT, never the body — the server uses the authenticated `driverId`, so you can't ping as someone else. Object-level authz scopes a driver app to its region and rate-limits it; ops dashboards get broader read scope.
670	🗣️ In plain words: we trust the login token, not the request fields, and we compute the cell ourselves so nobody can fake their location bucket.
671	💬 One-liner: "Identity from the token, cell from the server — the client can't forge either."
672	
673	**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?"**
674	The mechanism: CDN/tile egress (~$1.2M/mo) dwarfs everything — it's bytes served to every viewer. The biggest cut is shrinking the tile: delta-encode against the previous tile, quantize counts into buckets, gzip — roughly 3x smaller, cutting egress to ~$0.4M/mo.
675	🗣️ In plain words: the priciest thing is sending the map to millions of phones; making the map file smaller saves the most money.
676	💬 One-liner: "It's egress — shrink the tile with delta-encoding and you cut the top line threefold."
677	
678	**❓ "Why every 5 seconds and not real-time streaming to each client?"**
679	The mechanism: a driver can't act on sub-5s changes, and per-client streaming would re-personalize a shared artifact, collapsing the CDN economy and exploding cost. Five seconds is the knee where it still feels live.
680	🗣️ In plain words: faster refresh costs a lot more for freshness people can't even notice.
681	💬 One-liner: "Five seconds is the cheapest cadence that still feels live; faster just burns egress."
682	
683	**❓ "What if an entire region (cloud region) goes down?"**
684	The mechanism: aggregators and tile builders are stateless and rebuildable, so we fail over to a healthy region and replay from the (cross-region-replicated) log; the CDN keeps serving last-good tiles meanwhile. We accept a brief freshness gap, not an outage.
685	🗣️ In plain words: because nothing critical is stored only in one place, we just respawn elsewhere and the map keeps showing the last picture.
686	💬 One-liner: "Stateless + rebuildable means region loss is a respawn, not a recovery project."
687	
688	**❓ "How do you support multiple zoom levels efficiently?"**
689	The mechanism: S2 cells are hierarchical — a fine cell id rolls up to a coarser one by dropping bits. We store the finest needed resolution and the tile builder sums children for coarser zooms, so one set of counts serves all zooms.
690	🗣️ In plain words: zoom out just means grouping the little squares into bigger squares, which S2 does for free.
691	💬 One-liner: "S2's hierarchy lets one fine count roll up to every zoom level."
692	
693	**❓ "What stops the read path from stampeding origin every 5 seconds?"**
694	The mechanism: stale-while-revalidate serves the slightly-stale tile while one background fetch refreshes it; single-flight coalesces concurrent edge misses into one origin fetch; jittered TTLs desynchronize expiry. Origin sees a flat ~200/s, not pulses.
695	🗣️ In plain words: when the cached map goes stale we keep handing out the old one and quietly fetch one fresh copy, instead of everyone rushing the origin at once.
696	💬 One-liner: "Stale-while-revalidate plus single-flight turns a herd into one fetch."
697	
698	**❓ "How would you add rider-demand heat (not just driver supply)?"**
699	The mechanism: rider open-app and request events flow through the same firehose with an `available`/`demand` tag; the aggregator keeps a parallel demand counter per cell; the tile carries both `supply` and `demand`, and the client colors the ratio.
700	🗣️ In plain words: demand is just another set of dots counted in the same buckets — we already left room for it in the tile.
701	💬 One-liner: "Demand reuses the whole pipeline — one more counter per cell, one more field per tile."
702	
703	**❓ "How would you add per-vehicle-type heatmaps later?"**
704	The mechanism: that breaks the single-shared-tile assumption, so we'd precompute a *small fixed set* of tile variants per city (e.g. 3 service lines) — still cacheable, just 3x the tiles — rather than per-user tiles, which would kill caching. Or push filtering client-side if the client gets all cell types.
705	🗣️ In plain words: a handful of standard map versions stays cacheable; a custom map per person does not.
706	💬 One-liner: "A few precomputed variants stay CDN-friendly; per-user filtering is what breaks the cache."
707	
708	**❓ "What's your back-pressure story if aggregators fall behind?"**
709	The mechanism: the log is a bounded buffer; if consumer lag exceeds one window (~10s) we shed by dropping the *oldest* pings first (lossy-OK), protecting freshness of recent data, and alarm to add workers. We never block the gateway/drivers.
710	🗣️ In plain words: if we get behind, we throw away the stalest pings rather than the newest, because old positions are worthless anyway.
711	💬 One-liner: "Under overload we drop the oldest pings first — recent heat is the only thing worth keeping."
712	
713	**❓ "How do you know the heatmap is even right / observable?"**
714	The mechanism: golden signals — ingest QPS, read QPS, build latency, consumer lag, Redis saturation — with a trace-id stamped at the gateway and carried through the log and builder. SLO burn-rate alerts fire on stale tiles (build age > 15s) and lag > one window.
715	🗣️ In plain words: we watch how fresh the tiles are and how far behind the counters are, and we can follow one driver's ping all the way through.
716	💬 One-liner: "We alarm on tile staleness and consumer lag, and every ping carries a trace-id across the async hop."
717	
718	> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is the system that paints live demand bubbles on a ride-hail map. The design splits into two halves. The read half is almost a static-file CDN: a builder snapshots each city's density into one tiny shared tile every five seconds, and a CDN with stale-while-revalidate fans it out, so eight hundred thousand reads a second collapse into a couple hundred origin fetches — reads are nearly free. The write half is the hard half: a two-and-a-half-million-pings-a-second firehose buffered in a partitioned log and folded into in-memory windowed counts, where the real problem is hot dense cells downtown — solved by sub-sharding hot ranges and pre-aggregating pings into batched plus-N increments. The two halves connect through that tile builder, the only bridge between the write store and the read store. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: this is an approximate, shared, display artifact, so we trade exactness and personalization for throughput and near-free reads."

28	ACTIVE_DRIVERS_PEAK = 5_000_000     # globally online drivers at peak
29	PING_INTERVAL_S = 4                 # driver app pings location every 4s
30	CITIES = 1_000                      # active markets
31	DRIVERS_PER_BIG_CITY_PEAK = 50_000  # e.g. a NYC/Jakarta at rush hour
32	TILE_REFRESH_S = 5                  # heatmap recomputed every 5s
33	VIEWERS_PEAK = 2_000_000            # apps with the heatmap open at peak
34	HEATMAP_POLL_S = 5                  # client refreshes the tile every 5s
35	BURST_FACTOR = 2.0                  # peak-of-peak surge multiplier (rain, surge)
36	
37	print("\n--- 1. WRITE PATH: the location firehose ---")
38	# Every active driver emits a location ping on an interval.
39	avg_ping_qps = ACTIVE_DRIVERS_PEAK / PING_INTERVAL_S
40	peak_ping_qps = avg_ping_qps * BURST_FACTOR
41	print(f"avg ingest = {ACTIVE_DRIVERS_PEAK:,} drivers / {PING_INTERVAL_S}s "
42	      f"= {avg_ping_qps:,.0f}/s -> {r(avg_ping_qps)}/s")
43	print(f"PEAK ingest = avg x {BURST_FACTOR} burst = {peak_ping_qps:,.0f}/s "
44	      f"-> {r(peak_ping_qps)}/s   [THE FIREHOSE]")
45	
46	# Single hottest city write rate (the hot-shard concern)
47	hot_city_qps = DRIVERS_PER_BIG_CITY_PEAK / PING_INTERVAL_S * BURST_FACTOR
48	print(f"hottest single city = {DRIVERS_PER_BIG_CITY_PEAK:,}/{PING_INTERVAL_S}s "
49	      f"x {BURST_FACTOR} = {hot_city_qps:,.0f}/s -> {r(hot_city_qps)}/s")
50	
51	print("\n--- 2. GEO-BUCKETING: S2 cells ---")
52	# S2 cell sizes (approx edge length). We bucket pings into S2 cells.
53	# level 12 ~ 2-3 km^2, level 14 ~ 200 m, level 16 ~ 80m edge / ~0.006 km^2
54	# For a city heatmap tile we aggregate at ~level 13/14.
55	# How many active cells does a big city have at display resolution?
56	BIG_CITY_AREA_KM2 = 800            # metro area
57	CELL_AREA_KM2_L14 = 0.06           # ~level 14 cell footprint
58	active_cells_big_city = BIG_CITY_AREA_KM2 / CELL_AREA_KM2_L14
59	print(f"big city {BIG_CITY_AREA_KM2} km2 / {CELL_AREA_KM2_L14} km2 per cell "
60	      f"= {active_cells_big_city:,.0f} cells -> {r(active_cells_big_city)} cells")
61	# Globally, total occupied cells (sparse — drivers don't cover oceans)
62	GLOBAL_OCCUPIED_CELLS = 20_000_000   # order-of-mag of cells that ever see a driver
63	print(f"global occupied cells ~ {r(GLOBAL_OCCUPIED_CELLS)} (sparse, key space)")
64	
65	print("\n--- 3. READ PATH: precomputed tiles ---")
66	# Naive read QPS if every viewer polled origin directly.
67	naive_read_qps = VIEWERS_PEAK / HEATMAP_POLL_S
68	peak_read_qps = naive_read_qps * BURST_FACTOR
69	print(f"naive read = {VIEWERS_PEAK:,} viewers / {HEATMAP_POLL_S}s "
70	      f"= {naive_read_qps:,.0f}/s -> {r(naive_read_qps)}/s")
71	print(f"PEAK read = x {BURST_FACTOR} burst = {peak_read_qps:,.0f}/s "
72	      f"-> {r(peak_read_qps)}/s")
73	
74	# Size the origin from MISS QPS, not cache size.
75	# Tiles are tiny + identical for all viewers in a city -> CDN/edge cacheable
76	# with TTL == refresh interval. Hit ratio is very high.
77	HIT_RATIO = 0.99    # one tile per city, all viewers share it
78	miss_qps = peak_read_qps * (1 - HIT_RATIO)
79	print(f"with {HIT_RATIO:.0%} edge hit-ratio (one tile per city, shared):")
80	print(f"  MISS qps to origin = {peak_read_qps:,.0f} x {1-HIT_RATIO:.2f} "
81	      f"= {miss_qps:,.0f}/s -> {r(miss_qps)}/s")
82	# Cold-cache worst case (TTL expiry stampede, every city at once)
83	cold_qps = peak_read_qps
84	print(f"  cold-cache worst case = {cold_qps:,.0f}/s -> {r(cold_qps)}/s "
85	      f"(mitigate: single-flight + stale-while-revalidate)")
86	
87	# But the REAL read math: tiles are precomputed and pushed, so origin only
88	# serves one fresh tile per city per refresh, regardless of viewers.
89	tile_writes_qps = CITIES * (active_cells_big_city / active_cells_big_city)  # 1 tile/city avg unit
90	fresh_tiles_per_s = CITIES / TILE_REFRESH_S
91	print(f"fresh tile builds = {CITIES} cities / {TILE_REFRESH_S}s "
92	      f"= {fresh_tiles_per_s:,.0f} tiles/s -> {r(fresh_tiles_per_s)} tiles/s "
93	      f"(decoupled from {r(peak_read_qps)} reads)")
94	
95	print("\n--- 4. STORAGE: full footprint ---")
96	# Hot aggregate state: current count per occupied cell, kept in memory.
97	BYTES_PER_CELL_AGG = 24   # cell_id(8) + count(4) + demand(4) + ts(8)
98	hot_state_bytes = GLOBAL_OCCUPIED_CELLS * BYTES_PER_CELL_AGG
99	print(f"hot aggregate state = {r(GLOBAL_OCCUPIED_CELLS)} cells x {BYTES_PER_CELL_AGG}B "
100	      f"= {hot_state_bytes/1e9:.2f} GB -> under ~1 GB in memory (tiny -> fits one Redis)")
101	
102	# Precomputed tiles: one compact tile per city (binary, gzipped).
103	BYTES_PER_TILE = 30_000   # ~30 KB compact protobuf for a city tile
104	tile_set_bytes = CITIES * BYTES_PER_TILE
105	print(f"current tile set = {CITIES} cities x {BYTES_PER_TILE/1000:.0f}KB "
106	      f"= {tile_set_bytes/1e6:.0f} MB (trivial, fits in cache)")
107	
108	# Raw firehose if we PERSISTED every ping (the analytics/event-log table).
109	PING_BYTES = 64    # driver_id(8)+lat(8)+lon(8)+ts(8)+speed+heading+city+pad
110	raw_bytes_per_s = peak_ping_qps * PING_BYTES
111	raw_bytes_per_day = raw_bytes_per_s * 86_400
112	raw_bytes_per_month = raw_bytes_per_day * 30
113	REPL = 3
114	WAL_HEADROOM = 1.5
115	raw_footprint_month = raw_bytes_per_month * REPL * WAL_HEADROOM
116	print(f"IF we persisted raw pings: {r(peak_ping_qps)}/s x {PING_BYTES}B "
117	      f"= {raw_bytes_per_day/1e12:.1f} TB/day raw")
118	print(f"  x30 days x{REPL} repl x{WAL_HEADROOM} WAL = "
119	      f"{raw_footprint_month/1e12:.0f} TB/month -> we do NOT persist raw; we aggregate + drop")
120	
121	print("\n--- 5. BANDWIDTH / EGRESS ($ driver) ---")
122	# Read egress: tiles served to viewers (post-CDN, only misses + edge fill).
123	# Edge serves most; origin egress = misses.
124	egress_bytes_per_s = peak_read_qps * BYTES_PER_TILE   # if NOT cached (worst)
125	egress_cached_per_s = miss_qps * BYTES_PER_TILE        # realistic origin egress
126	print(f"client egress total = {r(peak_read_qps)}/s x {BYTES_PER_TILE/1000:.0f}KB "
127	      f"= {egress_bytes_per_s/1e9:.1f} GB/s served at edge")
128	egress_month_tb = egress_bytes_per_s * 86_400 * 30 / 1e12
129	print(f"  monthly edge egress = {egress_month_tb:,.0f} TB/month")
130	CDN_PER_GB = 0.02   # $/GB blended CDN egress
131	cdn_cost_month = egress_month_tb * 1000 * CDN_PER_GB
132	print(f"  CDN egress cost ~ {egress_month_tb:,.0f}TB x $0.02/GB "
133	      f"= ${cdn_cost_month/1e6:.1f}M/month -> {r(cdn_cost_month)}$/mo  [TOP COST]")
134	
135	print("\n--- 6. UNIT ECONOMICS ---")
136	reads_per_month = peak_read_qps * 86_400 * 30
137	cost_per_million_reads = cdn_cost_month / (reads_per_month / 1e6)
138	print(f"reads/month = {r(peak_read_qps)}/s x 2.6M s = {reads_per_month/1e9:.0f}B reads/mo")
139	print(f"$/1M reads (CDN) = ${cost_per_million_reads:.3f}  -> ~$0.{int(round(cost_per_million_reads*100)):02d} per 1M tile reads")
140	
141	print("\n--- 7. THE ONE DECISION-FORCING NUMBER ---")
142	print(f"FIREHOSE: {r(peak_ping_qps)} location pings/s into the aggregator.")
143	print(f"A single stream partition tops out ~50K msg/s; one aggregator core ~")
144	print(f"  ~100K-200K updates/s -> {peak_ping_qps:,.0f}/s FORCES sharding by S2 cell prefix.")
145	print(f"FLIP THRESHOLD: below ~150K pings/s a single Redis/aggregator handles it;")
146	print(f"  above that -> shard the firehose by cell-prefix across N aggregators.")
147	single_node_ceiling = 150_000
148	shards_needed = peak_ping_qps / single_node_ceiling
149	print(f"  shards needed = {peak_ping_qps:,.0f} / {single_node_ceiling:,} "
150	      f"= {shards_needed:.0f} aggregator shards -> round to ~{int(shards_needed*1.5)} with headroom")
151	
152	print("\n" + "=" * 64)
153	print("SUMMARY (talkable):")
154	print(f"  Firehose ingest (peak): {r(peak_ping_qps)}/s    [binding bottleneck]")
155	print(f"  Hottest city ingest:    {r(hot_city_qps)}/s     [hot shard]")
156	print(f"  Read demand (peak):     {r(peak_read_qps)}/s")
157	print(f"  Origin MISS qps:        {r(miss_qps)}/s         [size origin from this]")
158	print(f"  Fresh tile builds:      {r(fresh_tiles_per_s)} tiles/s")
159	print(f"  Hot state in memory:    {hot_state_bytes/1e9:.2f} GB (under 1 GB)")
160	print(f"  CDN egress cost:        {r(cdn_cost_month)}$/mo  [top cost driver]")
161	print(f"  $/1M reads:             ~$0.{int(round(cost_per_million_reads*100)):02d}")
162	print("=" * 64)
