================================================================
REAL-TIME DRIVER HEATMAP — CAPACITY MODEL
================================================================

--- 1. WRITE PATH: the location firehose ---
avg ingest = 5,000,000 drivers / 4s = 1,250,000/s -> ~1.2M/s
PEAK ingest = avg x 2.0 burst = 2,500,000/s -> ~2.5M/s   [THE FIREHOSE]
hottest single city = 50,000/4s x 2.0 = 25,000/s -> ~25K/s

--- 2. GEO-BUCKETING: S2 cells ---
big city 800 km2 / 0.06 km2 per cell = 13,333 cells -> ~13K cells
global occupied cells ~ ~20.0M (sparse, key space)

--- 3. READ PATH: precomputed tiles ---
naive read = 2,000,000 viewers / 5s = 400,000/s -> ~400K/s
PEAK read = x 2.0 burst = 800,000/s -> ~800K/s
with 99% edge hit-ratio (one tile per city, shared):
  MISS qps to origin = 800,000 x 0.01 = 8,000/s -> ~8K/s
  cold-cache worst case = 800,000/s -> ~800K/s (mitigate: single-flight + stale-while-revalidate)
fresh tile builds = 1000 cities / 5s = 200 tiles/s -> ~200 tiles/s (decoupled from ~800K reads)

--- 4. STORAGE: full footprint ---
hot aggregate state = ~20.0M cells x 24B = 0.48 GB -> under ~1 GB in memory (tiny -> fits one Redis)
current tile set = 1000 cities x 30KB = 30 MB (trivial, fits in cache)
IF we persisted raw pings: ~2.5M/s x 64B = 13.8 TB/day raw
  x30 days x3 repl x1.5 WAL = 1866 TB/month -> we do NOT persist raw; we aggregate + drop

--- 5. BANDWIDTH / EGRESS ($ driver) ---
client egress total = ~800K/s x 30KB = 24.0 GB/s served at edge
  monthly edge egress = 62,208 TB/month
  CDN egress cost ~ 62,208TB x $0.02/GB = $1.2M/month -> ~1.2M$/mo  [TOP COST]

--- 6. UNIT ECONOMICS ---
reads/month = ~800K/s x 2.6M s = 2074B reads/mo
$/1M reads (CDN) = $0.600  -> ~$0.60 per 1M tile reads

--- 7. THE ONE DECISION-FORCING NUMBER ---
FIREHOSE: ~2.5M location pings/s into the aggregator.
A single stream partition tops out ~50K msg/s; one aggregator core ~
  ~100K-200K updates/s -> 2,500,000/s FORCES sharding by S2 cell prefix.
FLIP THRESHOLD: below ~150K pings/s a single Redis/aggregator handles it;
  above that -> shard the firehose by cell-prefix across N aggregators.
  shards needed = 2,500,000 / 150,000 = 17 aggregator shards -> round to ~25 with headroom

================================================================
SUMMARY (talkable):
  Firehose ingest (peak): ~2.5M/s    [binding bottleneck]
  Hottest city ingest:    ~25K/s     [hot shard]
  Read demand (peak):     ~800K/s
  Origin MISS qps:        ~8K/s         [size origin from this]
  Fresh tile builds:      ~200 tiles/s
  Hot state in memory:    0.48 GB (under 1 GB)
  CDN egress cost:        ~1.2M$/mo  [top cost driver]
  $/1M reads:             ~$0.60
================================================================

=== idempotency ===
162:- Key decision: returns **202 Accepted**, fire-and-forget — we ack receipt, not durability. Dropping a ping is fine (NFR3); the next one is 4s away. No idempotency key needed because pings are *naturally idempotent by recency* — only the latest ping per driver per window counts, so a duplicate just overwrites itself.
279:1. **Read ping.** Worker pulls the next ping. *Why:* same consumer as 6.1. *State:* offset advances. *What if:* duplicate ping — harmless, recency-idempotent.
495:| Duplicate ping | Recency-idempotent — only latest window matters; double-count decays out in seconds |
666:💬 One-liner: "Recency-idempotent windows mean duplicates self-correct in seconds."
=== versioning/pagination ===
149:Default REST over HTTPS; everything under `/v1`. **Versioning:** additive fields (a new optional tile field) ship without a version bump; breaking changes (new tile encoding) get `/v2`. **Pagination:** the only list endpoint (multi-tile viewport) uses a cursor/keyset token over `(zoom, cell_range)` rather than offset/limit — offset re-scans and dupes as cells churn. **Security:** caller identity always comes from the JWT (driver/rider/ops), never a client-supplied id; object-level authz checks the caller may view the requested region (ops dashboards can see any city; a driver app is rate-limited and region-scoped).
=== authz ===
149:Default REST over HTTPS; everything under `/v1`. **Versioning:** additive fields (a new optional tile field) ship without a version bump; breaking changes (new tile encoding) get `/v2`. **Pagination:** the only list endpoint (multi-tile viewport) uses a cursor/keyset token over `(zoom, cell_range)` rather than offset/limit — offset re-scans and dupes as cells churn. **Security:** caller identity always comes from the JWT (driver/rider/ops), never a client-supplied id; object-level authz checks the caller may view the requested region (ops dashboards can see any city; a driver app is rate-limited and region-scoped).
153:- Key fields: `driverId` (from JWT, **not** the body — client can't spoof another driver), `lat`/`lon` (where), `ts` (client time, server clamps it), `available` (bool — only idle drivers count toward supply). Absent on purpose: any cell-id — the server computes the S2 cell, so the client can't forge bucket membership.
184:> 🎙️ Script: "Two endpoints carry the whole system. Drivers push location pings — I model it as POST but it's really a persistent stream — and I just 202 them, because losing one ping doesn't matter, the next is four seconds out. Reads are a plain GET for a city at a zoom level, and the magic is one cache header: max-age five seconds with stale-while-revalidate, so the CDN hands everyone the same tile and origin barely notices. Identity always comes from the JWT, and the server computes the cell itself so nobody can forge which bucket they're in."
669:The mechanism: caller identity comes from the JWT, never the body — the server uses the authenticated `driverId`, so you can't ping as someone else. Object-level authz scopes a driver app to its region and rate-limits it; ops dashboards get broader read scope.
=== CAP/PACELC ===
98:## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
100:I computed these with a small `capacity.py`; I'll surface only the few that drive a decision. Gloss as I go: **QPS** = queries (or events) per second; **TTL** = time-to-live, how long a cached value stays valid; **S2 cell** = Google's scheme for chopping the globe into addressable square-ish tiles at many zoom levels.
640:**One-line CAP/PACELC:** the core path is **AP** — under a partition we keep serving (possibly stale) tiles rather than blocking; and under PACELC, in normal operation we favor **latency (and cost) over consistency**, because a few seconds of staleness is the product, not a defect.
=== back-pressure / observability / rate-limit ===
149:Default REST over HTTPS; everything under `/v1`. **Versioning:** additive fields (a new optional tile field) ship without a version bump; breaking changes (new tile encoding) get `/v2`. **Pagination:** the only list endpoint (multi-tile viewport) uses a cursor/keyset token over `(zoom, cell_range)` rather than offset/limit — offset re-scans and dupes as cells churn. **Security:** caller identity always comes from the JWT (driver/rider/ops), never a client-supplied id; object-level authz checks the caller may view the requested region (ops dashboards can see any city; a driver app is rate-limited and region-scoped).
201:| Ingest stream | A durable, partitioned log that buffers the firehose so consumers can fall behind without loss | Decouples spiky 2.5M/s ingest from aggregators; partition by cell-prefix gives geographic locality | Not direct-to-Redis (a slow aggregator would back-pressure the gateway); not a DB (no DB takes 2.5M writes/s) | A few seconds of buffering latency; we tune retention short (minutes) |
210:> **Back-pressure** — when a slow consumer signals upstream to slow down rather than overflow. Here the log absorbs it so the gateway never has to block drivers.
389:   - **Write straight to Redis/DB** — a slow consumer would back-pressure all the way up to the driver connection; the log decouples that.
607:**Back-pressure (hottest async path = ingest log → aggregators):** the log is a bounded buffer; under overload aggregators shed by **dropping the oldest pings first** (lossy-OK by NFR3) rather than blocking the gateway — we protect freshness of *recent* data over completeness of old data. Trigger signal: consumer lag and queue depth; alarm at lag > 10s (one window).
609:**Observability:** golden signals — latency (read p99, build time), traffic (ingest QPS, read QPS), errors (gateway 5xx, build failures), saturation (consumer lag, Redis ops/s). A **trace-id** is stamped at the gateway and propagated through the log → aggregator → builder so one driver's ping is traceable across the async hop. Burn-rate alert on the read-path SLO at 2% budget/hour.
611:**Rate-limiting:** the public read endpoint uses a token-bucket per client/IP; over-limit returns **HTTP 429 with Retry-After**. The limiter state is itself a global coordinator (a shared Redis counter) — kept regional to avoid a cross-region hop.
669:The mechanism: caller identity comes from the JWT, never the body — the server uses the authenticated `driverId`, so you can't ping as someone else. Object-level authz scopes a driver app to its region and rate-limits it; ops dashboards get broader read scope.

All §5/§9/§10 elements present. Now let me scrutinize technical correctness more deeply. A few claims to verify adversarially:

1. **Pre-aggregation "~40x" / "drivers-per-cell factor" claim** (line 487): The claim is that batching N pings into one +N reduces central writes by the drivers-per-cell factor (~40x for a 40-driver cell). But this conflates two things. Let me think: if a worker batches one second of pings per cell, the reduction factor is *pings-per-cell-per-batch-window*, not drivers-per-cell. With a 1s batch window and 4s ping interval, each driver pings ~0.25 times per second, so a 40-driver cell gets ~10 pings/sec, not 40. So one +N per second would collapse ~10 pings → 1, i.e. ~10x, not 40x. The "40x" assumes all 40 drivers ping within the same batch window, which only holds if the batch window ≥ ping interval (4s). This is a mild overstatement but the mechanism is sound. Let me note this as a MINOR.

2. **§5 idempotency claim** "No idempotency key needed because pings are naturally idempotent by recency." This is reasonable for the firehose given the lossy-OK model. Acceptable.

3. **Hot cell vs hot partition**: The deep dive correctly distinguishes hot city (25K/s) from a single hot cell, and the sub-sharding by `(cellPrefix, hash(driverId) % k)`. But there's a subtle issue — if you sub-shard a single hot *cell* across k partitions/workers, each worker now has a *partial* count for that cell, so you need a final merge step to sum them before the tile builder reads it. The doc says "downtown spreads over k partitions/workers" — for a cell-*prefix* (range of cells) this is fine since different cells land on different workers. But for a single hot *cell* (stadium), splitting by `hash(driverId) % k` means k workers each hold a partial count and someone must sum them. The doc doesn't explicitly address the re-merge. Let me check the surrounding text.

Let me look at the deep dive and the local pre-aggregation math claim more carefully.

content.text:
Now I have a clear picture of the technical claims. Let me assess the pre-aggregation math claim more rigorously:

The claim: "in a dense cell with ~40 drivers, 40 `+1`s become one `+40` — a ~40x reduction... batches one second of pings per cell."

The problem: with a **1-second** batch window and a **4-second** ping interval, in any given 1-second window only ~1/4 of the 40 drivers (i.e. ~10) emit a ping. So a 1s batch collapses ~10 pings → 1 emission = ~10x reduction, not 40x. The "40x" only holds if the batch window equals the ping interval (4s) so that all 40 drivers' pings fall in one batch. The text explicitly says "batches one second of pings" then claims 40x — these are inconsistent. The *direction* and mechanism are correct (pre-aggregation collapses N pings to one +N), but the stated 40x reduction is internally inconsistent with the 1-second batch window. This is a MINOR (false-precision/internal inconsistency), not a blocker — the qualitative point stands.

A second, subtler technical gap: **sub-sharding a single hot cell** via `hash(driverId) % k` distributes one cell's pings across k workers, so each worker holds only a *partial* count for that cell. The tile builder (or a merge step) must then **sum the k partial counts** to get the true cell density. The doc never mentions this re-merge step — it implies the count is just "spread." For a hot cell-*prefix* (a range of distinct cells) there's no problem since each cell lives on one worker; but the failure matrix explicitly lists "Hot cell (stadium out)" → "Sub-shard split across k partitions," which is the single-cell case that *does* need re-merge. This is a real but minor gap in completeness, not a wrong claim. I'll note it as MINOR.
